_comment: DRAFT — Lower back stability exercises. Not yet clinically reviewed. Do not integrate into runtime code.
_condition: Lumbar spine stability — lower back pain, disc injury, general core rehabilitation

exercises.name: Posterior Pelvic Tilt
exercises.description: Lying on your back with knees bent, gently flatten your lower back into the floor by tightening your abdominals and tilting the pelvis. The foundational exercise for activating the core and reducing lumbar lordosis. Often the first exercise prescribed for lower back pain.
exercises.cue: Imagine pressing your lower back like a stamp into the floor. Do not hold your breath — breathe normally.
exercises.painNote: This exercise should reduce or centralise lower back pain, not increase it. Stop if symptoms worsen.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying on back with knees bent, flattening the lower back into the floor
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to heel slides or single-leg march while maintaining pelvic tilt.

exercises.name: McGill Curl-Up
exercises.description: Lie on your back with one knee bent and one leg flat. Place your hands under your lower back to maintain its natural curve. Lift only your head and shoulders off the floor — do not flex through the lumbar spine. Based on Stuart McGill's research into safe spinal flexion.
exercises.tempo: Slow lift — hold — lower with control
exercises.cue: Think of lengthening your neck, not tucking your chin. Your lower back should stay in its natural curve — not flat.
exercises.painNote: Traditional crunches flex the lumbar spine under load. This exercise deliberately avoids that. If you feel lower back pain during the hold, check your hand position under the lumbar spine.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying with hands under lower back, lifting head and shoulders slightly while spine stays neutral
contraindications: acute lumbar disc herniation with flexion-biased symptoms
exercises.progressionNotes: Increase hold duration to 10 s once 8×8 s is comfortable.

exercises.name: Side Plank (McGill Modified)
exercises.description: Lie on your side, supported on your forearm and knees (modified) or feet (full). Lift the hips to form a straight line and hold. Trains lateral core stability — specifically quadratus lumborum and obliques — without compressing the lumbar discs.
exercises.cue: Hips forward — do not let them sag backward. Your body should form a straight line from head to heels (or knees).
exercises.painNote: Lower back pain during side plank — check you are not rotating the pelvis forward or backward. Return to the modified (knee-supported) version.
exercises.imageAlt: Person in side plank position supported on forearm and feet, body forming a straight line
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress from knee-supported to full side plank. Then add hip dips or leg raises.

exercises.name: Prone Press-Up (McKenzie)
exercises.description: Lying face down, press up onto your hands while allowing the lower back to extend. The hips remain on the floor. A McKenzie extension exercise used to centralise disc-related pain and restore lumbar extension range.
exercises.tempo: Press up slowly — hold — lower with control
exercises.cue: Let your lower back relax and sag — do not tighten it. Your arms are just propping you up.
exercises.painNote: This exercise should reduce or centralise lower back pain. If symptoms spread further into the leg, stop and contact your physiotherapist.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying face down pressing up onto hands while lower back sags into extension
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to standing lumbar extension once prone press-up is comfortable.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.description: On hands and knees, alternate between rounding the spine toward the ceiling (cat) and allowing it to sag toward the floor (cow). A gentle movement warm-up that restores full lumbar flexion and extension range.
exercises.tempo: Slow and rhythmic — 3 s in each direction
exercises.cue: Move through the full available range in both directions. Breathe in as you sag down (cow), breathe out as you round up (cat).
exercises.painNote: If one direction is painful, stay within the comfortable range only. Report consistent pain in one direction to your coach.
exercises.imageAlt: Person on all fours alternating between rounding and arching the spine
exercises.progressionNotes: Pair with thread-the-needle for thoracic rotation mobility.

exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.description: Stand sideways to a cable machine or anchored resistance band. Hold the handle at chest height with both hands. Press straight out, hold, and return. The band tries to rotate you — resisting this rotation is the training stimulus. A pure anti-rotation core exercise.
exercises.cue: Your hips and shoulders should stay square throughout. The only movement is your arms pressing out.
exercises.painNote: If you feel lower back strain, check your foot width and reduce the band tension.
exercises.imageAlt: Person standing sideways to a resistance band anchor, pressing hands straight out from chest
exercises.progressionNotes: Increase band resistance. Progress to half-kneeling Pallof press for greater instability.

exercises.name: Superman / Back Extension
exercises.description: Lying face down on a mat, simultaneously lift the arms and legs off the floor and hold. Strengthens the erector spinae and posterior chain. A simple bodyweight posterior chain exercise often used early in lower back rehabilitation.
exercises.cue: Reach long through the fingertips and toes. Keep your neck neutral — do not crane upward.
exercises.painNote: If lumbar extension causes or increases lower back pain, regress to alternating arm/leg lifts. Avoid with extension-biased lumbar stenosis.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying face down lifting both arms and both legs simultaneously off the floor
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to back extension on a GHD machine or Swiss ball.

exercises.name: Dead Bug with Resistance Band
exercises.description: Standard dead bug with a resistance band looped around both feet and anchored overhead. The band creates pull toward extension — the core must resist this throughout. A significant progression from the bodyweight dead bug.
exercises.cue: The band is pulling your arms toward the floor — resist that pull with your deep core, not your hip flexors.
exercises.painNote: If your lower back arches even slightly off the floor during the movement, regress to bodyweight dead bug.
exercises.imageAlt: Person in dead bug position with resistance band adding tension to the movement
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to pallof press in a split stance.
